11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}

13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement

127	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
128	\begin{definition}
129	\leanok
130	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
131	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
133	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
134	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
135	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
137	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
138	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
139	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
140	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
141	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
142	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
143	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
144	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
146	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
147	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
148	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
149	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
150	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
151	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
152	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
153	  the Mathlib predicate
154	  \[
155	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
156	  \]
157	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
158	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
159	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
160	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
161	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
162	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
163	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
164	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
165	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
166	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
167	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
168	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
169	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
170	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
171	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
172	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
173	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
174	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
175	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
176	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
177	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
178	  \begin{itemize}
179	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
180	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
181	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
182	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
183	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
184	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
185	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
186	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
187	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
188	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
189	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
190	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
191	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
192	      uses.
193	  \end{itemize}
194	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
195	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
196	  needs.
197	\end{definition}

229	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
230	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
231	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
232	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
233	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.

246	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130 (the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)).}
247	  A scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in codimension one} when for every point
248	  \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) (equivalently,
249	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1\)) the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is
250	  a discrete valuation ring. The Lean encoding bundles this conjunction as
251	  a one-field \texttt{Prop} class so that downstream order / principal /
252	  degree lemmas may consume it as a typeclass hypothesis.
253	\end{definition}
254	\section{Open-immersion descent for prime divisors}
255	\label{sec:open_immersion_descent}
256	\textit{Source: Stacks Project tags 02IZ (stalk under open immersion) +
257	01J6 (open immersion preserves regularity); cross-referenced with the
258	project-local iter-183 substrate
259	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}.}
260	
261	The chapter's order / principal-divisor API is naturally stated on the
262	ambient scheme \(X\), but several downstream proofs (the
263	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) non-zero branch in
264	particular) require localising to an affine open \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
265	identifying prime divisors of \(X\) lying in \(U\) with prime divisors of
266	\(U\), and transporting stalk-level data along the open-immersion stalk
267	isomorphism. This section records the substrate bijection
268	\(\{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
269	 \;\simeq\; U.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\)
270	and the open-immersion descent of the \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
271	class.

546	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
547	\begin{definition}
548	\leanok
549	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
550	  \label{def:order_at_point}
551	  
552	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
554	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
555	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
556	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
557	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
559	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
560	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
561	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
562	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
564	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
565	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
566	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
567	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
568	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
569	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
570	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
571	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
572	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
573	  \[
574	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
575	  \]
576	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
577	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
578	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
579	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
580	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
581	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
582	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
583	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
584	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
585	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
586	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
587	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
588	  \begin{quote}
589	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
590	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
591	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
592	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
593	  \end{quote}
594	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
595	  see below). The body is
596	  \[
597	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
598	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
599	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
600	      \bigr).
601	  \]
602	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
603	  \begin{itemize}
604	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
605	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
606	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
607	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
608	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
609	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
610	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
611	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
612	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
613	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
614	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
615	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
616	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
617	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
618	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
619	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
620	      X.functionField} via
621	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
622	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
623	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
624	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
625	      and from the explicitly threaded
626	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
627	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
628	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
629	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
630	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
631	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
632	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
633	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
634	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
635	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
636	  \end{itemize}
637	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
638	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
639	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the

1039	\section{Positive part of a Weil divisor}
1040	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
1041	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
1042	% (iter-189). `Hom.poleDivisor` is being refactored to return the
1043	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
1044	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
1045	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
1046	% positive part can have positive degree).
1047	\begin{definition}
1048	
1049	
1050	\leanok
1051	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
1052	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
1053	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
1054	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
1055	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
1056	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
1057	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
1058	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
1059	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
1060	  \[
1061	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
1062	  \]
1063	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
1064	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
1065	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
1066	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
1067	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
1068	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
1069	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
1070	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
1071	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
1072	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
1073	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
1074	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
1075	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
1076	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
1077	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
1078	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
1079	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
1080	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
1081	\end{definition}

1116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
1117	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
1118	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
1119	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
1120	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
1121	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
1122	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
1123	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
1124	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
1125	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
1126	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1127	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
1128	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
1129	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
1130	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
1131	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
1132	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
1133	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
1134	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
1135	  extension degree:
1136	  \[
1137	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
1138	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
1139	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1140	  \]
1141	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
1142	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
1143	\end{lemma}
1144	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
1145	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
1146	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
1147	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
1148	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
1149	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
1150	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
1151	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
1152	\begin{proof}
1153	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
1154	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
1155	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
1156	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
1157	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
1158	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
1159	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
1160	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
1161	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
1162	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
1163	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
1164	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
1165	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
1166	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
1167	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
1168	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
1169	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
1170	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
1171	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
1172	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
1173	  \[
1174	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
1175	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
1176	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
1177	  \]
1178	  where the second equality uses
1179	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
1180	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
1181	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
1182	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
1183	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
1184	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
1185	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
1186	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
1187	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
1188	  \[
1189	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
1190	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1191	  \]
1192	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
1193	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
1194	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
1195	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
1196	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
1197	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
1198	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
1199	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
1200	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
1201	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
1202	  Therefore
1203	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
1204	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
1205	  taking degree completes the proof.
1206	\end{proof}
1207	\section{Divisor class group}
1208	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
1209	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
1210	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
1211	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
1212	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
1213	out of scope here.
1214	\begin{definition}
1215	\leanok
1216	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
1217	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
1218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
1219	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom, def:codim1_cycles}
1220	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
1221	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
1222	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
1223	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
1224	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
1225	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
1226	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
1227	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
1228	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
1229	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
1230	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
1231	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
1232	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
1233	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
1234	  \[
1235	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
1236	  \]
1237	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
1238	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
1239	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
1240	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
1241	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
1242	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
1243	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
1244	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
1245	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
1246	\end{definition}
1247	\section{Out of scope}
1248	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
1249	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
1250	\begin{itemize}
1251	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
1252	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
1253	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
1254	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
1255	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
1256	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
1257	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
1258	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
1259	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
1260	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1261	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
1262	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
1263	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
1264	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
1265	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
1266	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
1267	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
1268	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
1269	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
1270	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
1271	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
1272	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
1273	    strictly curve-focused.
1274	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
1275	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
1276	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
1277	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
1278	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
1279	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
1280	\end{itemize}

249	  have heq : Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S) = Ideal.map (e : R →+* S) (Ideal.span {x}) := by
250	    rw [Ideal.map_span, Set.image_singleton]; rfl
251	  letI alg : Algebra R S := e.toRingHom.toAlgebra
252	  haveI : IsScalarTower R S (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) := by
253	    refine ⟨fun r s m => ?_⟩
254	    change (algebraMap R S r * s) • m = (algebraMap R S r) • (s • m)
255	    rw [mul_smul]
256	  let φ_ring : (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) ≃+* (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) :=
257	    Ideal.quotientEquiv (Ideal.span {x}) (Ideal.span {e x}) e heq
258	  let φ : (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) ≃ₗ[R] (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) := by
259	    refine { φ_ring.toAddEquiv with map_smul' := ?_ }
260	    intro r m
261	    induction m using Quotient.inductionOn with
262	    | h y =>
263	      change Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (e (r * y)) = e r • Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (e y)
264	      rw [map_mul]; rfl
265	  unfold Ring.ord
266	  rw [φ.length_eq]
267	  exact Module.length_eq_of_surjective (S := R) (R := S) (M := S ⧸ Ideal.span {e x})
268	    e.surjective

273	private lemma nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
274	    (e : R ≃+* S) (r : R) :

276	  rw [← MulEquivClass.map_nonZeroDivisors (e : R ≃* S)]
277	  refine ⟨fun h => ⟨r, h, rfl⟩, fun h => ?_⟩
278	  obtain ⟨r', hr', heq⟩ := h

290	  by_cases hr : r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R
291	  · have her : e r ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e r).mp hr
292	    simp [hr, her, ord_ringEquiv e r]

298	(per PROGRESS.md L108--L122 recipe step (1)).
299	
300	Given a ring iso `e : R ≃+* S` between Noetherian Krull-dim-`≤ 1` integral
301	domains and a compatible fraction-field iso `e_K : K_R ≃+* K_S` (compatibility
302	`e_K (algebraMap R K_R r) = algebraMap S K_S (e r)` for `r : R`), the
303	order-of-vanishing function on the fraction fields is invariant:
304	`ordFrac S (e_K x) = ordFrac R x` for any `x : K_R`.
305	
306	Proof: `x = 0` is the junk-zero branch. For `x ≠ 0`, write `x = mk' K_R a b`
307	with `a, b ∈ nonZeroDivisors R` via `IsLocalization.surj`. Then
308	`e_K x = mk' K_S (e a) (e b)` via the compatibility hypothesis, and both
309	sides reduce via Mathlib's `ordFrac_eq_div` to a quotient of
310	`ordMonoidWithZeroHom`-values, which agree by `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv`. -/
311	private lemma ordFrac_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsDomain R]
312	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]
313	    [CommRing S] [IsDomain S] [IsNoetherianRing S] [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 S]
314	    {K_R K_S : Type*} [Field K_R] [Field K_S]
315	    [Algebra R K_R] [IsFractionRing R K_R]
316	    [Algebra S K_S] [IsFractionRing S K_S]
317	    (e : R ≃+* S) (e_K : K_R ≃+* K_S)
318	    (h_compat : ∀ r : R, e_K (algebraMap R K_R r) = algebraMap S K_S (e r))
319	    (x : K_R) :
320	    Ring.ordFrac S (e_K x) = Ring.ordFrac R x := by
321	  by_cases hx : x = 0
322	  · subst hx; rw [map_zero, map_zero, map_zero]
323	  obtain ⟨⟨a, b⟩, hab⟩ := IsLocalization.surj (nonZeroDivisors R) x
324	  have hb_nzd : (b : R) ∈ nonZeroDivisors R := b.2
325	  have hb_ne : (b : R) ≠ 0 := mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff_ne_zero.mp hb_nzd
326	  have h_inj_R : Function.Injective (algebraMap R K_R) := IsFractionRing.injective R K_R
327	  have hb_K_ne : algebraMap R K_R (b : R) ≠ 0 := by
328	    rw [Ne, ← map_zero (algebraMap R K_R), h_inj_R.eq_iff]
329	    exact hb_ne
330	  have ha_ne : a ≠ 0 := by
331	    intro ha
332	    subst ha
333	    rw [map_zero] at hab
334	    rcases mul_eq_zero.mp hab with hx' | hb_ne'
335	    · exact hx hx'
336	    · exact hb_K_ne hb_ne'
337	  have ha_nzd : a ∈ nonZeroDivisors R := mem_nonZeroDivisors_of_ne_zero ha_ne
338	  have he_b_nzd : e b ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e b).mp hb_nzd
339	  have he_a_nzd : e a ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e a).mp ha_nzd
340	  have hx_mk : x = IsLocalization.mk' K_R a ⟨b, hb_nzd⟩ := by
341	    rw [eq_comm, IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq_mul, hab]
342	  have he_K_x : e_K x = IsLocalization.mk' K_S (e a) ⟨e b, he_b_nzd⟩ := by
343	    rw [eq_comm]
344	    refine (IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq_mul).mpr ?_
345	    rw [← h_compat a, ← h_compat b, ← map_mul]
346	    exact congrArg e_K hab.symm
347	  rw [he_K_x, hx_mk]
348	  rw [Ring.ordFrac_eq_div S ⟨e a, he_a_nzd⟩ ⟨e b, he_b_nzd⟩]
349	  rw [Ring.ordFrac_eq_div R ⟨a, ha_nzd⟩ ⟨b, hb_nzd⟩]
350	  congr 1
351	  · exact ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv e a
352	  · exact ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv e b

392	
393	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
394	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational

399	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
400	
401	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
402	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
403	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
404	discrete valuation on `f`.

406	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
407	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.

409	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
